coredata.dc:title: Flexible polyaniline/carbon quantum dots films for enhanced electrochromic performance
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Ting
Deng, Lianwen
Zhang, Ningrong
Zhang, Pin
Liu, Yawen
Qin, Longlong
Huang, Shengxiang
Qiu, Leilei
Peng, Sen
coredata.dc:description: Polyaniline (PANI) conducting polymers exhibit potential electrochromic properties. However, the electrochromic response of PANI films in the infrared (IR) ranges is restricted by their dense structure. Herein, the polyaniline/carbon quantum dots (PANI/CQDs) films were synthesized with sulfuric acid (SA) and CQDs as the dopants. The tetrahedral structures of SA contribute to the formation of highly oriented lamellar PANI. The synergistic effect between CQDs and PANI promotes the loose and porous surface morphology, thereby improving the electrochromic performance of PANI film. The PANI/CQDs porous film for the polymerization charge of 0.5C (PANI/CQDs-5) shows the optimal electrochromic properties and IR modulation capacity, achieving an optical contrast (ΔT) values of 53 % at 850 nm, and with the emittance variation (Δε) values of 0.41, 0.46 and 0.42 in 3–5 μm, 8–14 μm, and 2.5–25 μm, respectively. Furthermore, the switching time of the PANI/CQDs-5 films for coloration and blanching are 2.5 s and 3.6 s, respectively, with over 95 % retention after 100 cycles. Theoretical calculations verified that PANI/CQDs composite films displayed outstanding electron and ion transfer kinetics, facilitating superior electrochromic properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochromic materials
Polyaniline
Carbon quantum dots
Infrared emissivity
Emissive power

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: High resolution TEM images of (a) CQDs (the white round frames show tiny CQDs with a lattice spacing of 0.338 nm), and (b) PANI/CQDs-5 films; (c) HRTEM images from (b). SEM images of surface on (d) PANI-5 and (e) PANI/CQDs-5 porous films. (f) The porosity and average pore sizes of prepared films. The cross-section of (g) PANI-5 and (h) PANI/CQDs-5 porous films. (i) SEM images of partial enlargement of the marked area. (j) The XRD spectra of Au/PES substrate, CQDs, PANI/CQDs-5 and PANI-5 porous films. (k) SAED pattern of PANI/CQDs-5 porous films. (l) UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs, PANI-5 and PANI/CQDs-5.
FTIR spectra (a) and Raman spectra (b) of the CQDs, PANI/CQDs-5 and PANI-5 porous films. The XPS spectra of (c) C 1s and (d) N 1s for PANI/CQDs-5 and PANI-5 porous films.

Electrochemical properties: CV curves of (a) PANI/CQDs, (b) PANI, (c) PANI/CQDs-5 and PANI-5 porous films measured in 0.5 M SA aqueous solutions; (d) EIS of PANI/CQDs and PANI films measured in 0.5 M SA aqueous solutions. (Inset is Nyquist plots of the films in the low frequency region, and equivalent circuit model).

Optimized structure of (a) PANI and (b) PANI/CQDs system. Carbon atoms are brown, nitrogen atoms are blue, oxygen atoms are red and hydrogen atoms are pink. H ions migration is light blue. (c) Energy profiles of H-ion migration. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Emittance curves for (a) PANI/CQDs-4, (b) PANI/CQDs-5, (c) PANI/CQDs-6, (d) PANI-4; (e) PANI-5, and (f) PANI-6 films at −0.25 V and 0.55 V.

(a) Photographs of PANI/CQDs electrochromic film with the size of 1 × 1 cm2 under different voltages, suggesting the ability of rich color modulation. (b) Reflectance spectra and (c) CIE color coordinates. (d) The response time of PANI/CQDs-5 film and PANI-5 film at 0.55V and −0.25V. (e) The current stability of PANI/CQDs-5 film during 100 redox cycles. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of the fabrication process and 3D structures for the PANI/CQDs and PANI porous films.

Δε
a of the prepared films in the different wavelength ranges.

head.ce:title: Flexible polyaniline/carbon quantum dots films for enhanced electrochromic performance

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Key Laboratory on Electromagnetic Environmental Effects and Electro-optical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Key Laboratory on Electromagnetic Environmental Effects and Electro-optical Engineering, Army Engineering University of PLA, Nanjing 210007, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polyaniline (PANI) conducting polymers exhibit potential electrochromic properties. However, the electrochromic response of PANI films in the infrared (IR) ranges is restricted by their dense structure. Herein, the polyaniline/carbon quantum dots (PANI/CQDs) films were synthesized with sulfuric acid (SA) and CQDs as the dopants. The tetrahedral structures of SA contribute to the formation of highly oriented lamellar PANI. The synergistic effect between CQDs and PANI promotes the loose and porous surface morphology, thereby improving the electrochromic performance of PANI film. The PANI/CQDs porous film for the polymerization charge of 0.5C (PANI/CQDs-5) shows the optimal electrochromic properties and IR modulation capacity, achieving an optical contrast (ΔT) values of 53 % at 850 nm, and with the emittance variation (Δε) values of 0.41, 0.46 and 0.42 in 3–5 μm, 8–14 μm, and 2.5–25 μm, respectively. Furthermore, the switching time of the PANI/CQDs-5 films for coloration and blanching are 2.5 s and 3.6 s, respectively, with over 95 % retention after 100 cycles. Theoretical calculations verified that PANI/CQDs composite films displayed outstanding electron and ion transfer kinetics, facilitating superior electrochromic properties.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochromic materials with reversibly changeable optical properties, such as transmittance, absorption and emittance, by the redox reaction under the external voltage, have provided new possibilities for smart windows, sensors, color displays, supercapacitors, infrared (IR) optical switches, and adaptive thermal camouflage [1–5]. The general electrochromic materials include transition metal oxides (WO3) [6,7], and conducting polymers (polyaniline, polypyrrole, and polythiophene) [4,8–10]. Recently, conducting polymers based on polyaniline (PANI) have attracted much attention due to their wide band tuneability, fast response time, and low materials cost [11–16]. Especially in the IR field, some applications based on PANI have become true for the high IR contrast tunability [17]. However, the practical electrochromic properties of PANI, notably optical contrast in IR range and switching speed, still need to be further optimized. The problem with PANI materials in electrochromic behavior is that polymer chains are aggregated together to form a dense structure, which hinders electrolyte infiltration and ion transmission of PANI films [18].

Dopants with three-dimensional (3D) structures and relatively more active sites, such as protonic acids, have been verified to enhance the electrochromic performance of polyaniline systems [19,20]. For example, PANI films featuring 3D ordered microporous structures can be achieved via template-guided electrochemical polymerization [21]. Liu et al. [22] reported that PANI films with SA dopants could establish ionic and hydrogen bonds between adjacent molecular chains, inducing the formation of highly-aligned lamellar PANI crystals. The electrochromic performance of PANI doped with SA still needs to be further improved, potentially stemming from its compact surface structure that could hinder electrochemical reactions [20]. It is extremely important to propose a new strategy and dissect the IR electrochromic mechanism of the PANI films.

In this regard, carbonaceous materials with more active sites and high electrical conductivity are hopeful dopants to improve the electrochromic performances of PANI. Many reports ascribed the improved electrochemical properties to the congenerous effect between carbonaceous materials and PANI [23–28]. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted much attention due to their high electrical conductivity and large surface area [29–31]. Employing CQDs materials in the PANI is a reliable strategy for electrochromic applications, integrating virtues of short diffusion distance and large surface area for quick electrochromic response [32]. Thus, it is valuable to study on synergistic effect between CQDs and PANI for IR electrochromic performance.

In this work, the structure of PANI/CQDs was regulated by using sulfuric acid (SA) and CQDs as dopants. The prepared PANI/CQDs porous film exhibited commendable electrochromic properties, as evidenced by electrochemical measurements, as well as morphology and structure analysis. By adjusting the doping content of CQDs and polymerization charge of PANI/CQDs films, electrochromic performance, and IR emissivity modulation capacity were improved. The optimized PANI/CQDs films achieved ΔT of 53 % at 850 nm, Δε values of 0.41, 0.46 and 0.42 in ranges of 3–5 μm, 8–14 μm and 2.5–25 μm, respectively. Furthermore, the PANI/CQDs porous films showed a faster response rate and better stability. It is believed that the reported films will contribute to IR electrochromic devices in infrared thermal control applications.
Aniline, sulfuric acid (SA), citric acid and ethanediamine were purchased from Aladdin Company. Details of other main materials used are in the Supporting Information.

The synthesis of CQDs [33] was detailed in Supporting Information. PANI/CQDs films were synthesized on the Au/PES microporous membrane (1 × 1 cm2) by electro-polymerization process. First, 0.5 M aniline monomer and 200 μL of CQDs aqueous solution (30 mg/mL, 40 mg/mL, and 50 mg/mL) were added in 1 M H2SO4 solution with magnetic stirring to guarantee the homogeneity solution. Then, the Au/PES microporous membrane was inserted into the above aqueous solution as the counter electrode, and the Ag/AgCl was used as the reference electrode. The chronoamperometry method was employed in the electro-polymerization process with the polymerization voltage of 0.7 V and the Au/PES microporous membrane was polymerized at the different charges of 0.4, 0.5 and 0.6 C/cm2. In the end, the fabricated films were cleaned with ultra-pure water and dried in the oven at room-temperature. The resulting products were marked as PANI/CQDs-4, PANI/CQDs-5, and PANI/CQDs-6 based on the polymerization charge, respectively. For comparison, comitans experiments PANI-4, PANI-5, and PANI-6 were also prepared by the uniform synthesis route as PANI/CQDs except without the CQDs.

To investigate the surface morphology of the prepared films and CQDs, advanced characterization techniques such as Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) were employed. The roughness of the prepared films was examined with an atomic force microscope (AFM 5500, Agilent Technologies). The porosity and average pore sizes were estimated using ImageJ software. The UV–vis absorption spectrum (UV2450-visible spectrophotometer, Shimadzu, Japan) was employed to explore the absorbance of CQDs and prepared films to visible light. The crystal structure of PANI/CQDs and PANI films was analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) by using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm). The conductivities of films were employed to measure by the standard four-probe method (Four-point Probe Meter, SDY-4D, China) at room temperature with pellet samples. Raman spectra were tested to study the molecular configuration of the prepared sample at the exciting wavelength of 532 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted to investigate the combined valence state of each element.

The electrochemical properties of prepared films were tested in a three-electrode system. In this configuration, a graphite rod and an Ag/AgCl electrode were respectively designated as the counter and reference electrodes, operating in a 0.5 M H2SO4 aqueous electrolyte solution. The evaluation encompassed a range of tests, including Cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and chronoamperometric (CA), all conducted efficiently using the CHI760E electrochemical workstation. A UV–vis–NIR spectrophotometer (UV3600 Plus, Shimadzu) was employed to characterize the electrochromic behavior at ambient temperature. Additionally, the International Commission on Illumination (CIE) 1931 color space was assessed utilizing a distinct spectrophotometer model (3nh, TS7700). Furthermore, the Nicolet IS50 Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) instrument was leveraged to analyze both the transmittance spectra and IR reflectance properties of the PANI/CQDs and PANI films across varying potentials.

Utilizing the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) within the framework of Density functional theory (DFT), calculations were executed via the Vienna ab initio Simulation Package (VASP), incorporating the Enzerhof (PBE) formulation and the projector Augmented Wave (PAW) method [34–36]. The energy cut-off for plane waves was specified at 520 eV, while the Brillouin zone integration was achieved through a Monkhorst-Pack mesh with 1 × 1 × 1 K-points. The iteration process converged when both the maximal residual force and the energy change fell below 0.03 eV Å−1 and 10−5 eV, respectively. Additionally, the migration barriers for H ions within the structures were estimated by employing the Climbing Image-Nudged Elastic Band methods. Furthermore, the stability of CQDs on the PANI surface was quantified by calculating the adsorption energy (Eads), defined as:
(1)
E
ads = E
PANI/CQDs
– (E
PANI + E
CQDs )
where E
PANI/CQDs is the total energy of the composite, while E
PANI and E
CQDs denote the total energy of isolated PANI polymer and CQDs, respectively.

The synthesis process of prepared films is illustrated in Scheme 1 . The films were prepared on the Au/PES microporous substrates via electrochemical polymerization (Fig. S1, Supporting Information) with SAas a dopant. The area of PANI/CQDs film is 1 × 1 cm2, the thickness is 0.1 mm, and the film is flexible and bendable (Fig. S2). During the optimization process for the concentration of CQDs solution, a range of products were characterized using CV and CA tests, as well as conductivity assessment (Fig. S4 and Table S1, Supporting Information). The impact of the CQDs concentration on the CV test of the resulting composites was examined in Fig. S3. Notably, the oxidation peaks of PANI/CQDs-2 film shift to lower voltage, while the reduction peaks move towards higher voltages, indicating superior electrical conductivity compared to other films. Data presented in Table S1 further indicated that the PANI/CQDs-2 film, synthesized with 8 mg of CQDs (200 μL, 40 mg/mL), exhibited the optimized infrared electrochromic properties. Additionally, the doping ratio of CQDs in the PANI/CQDs films was determined to be 1.2 % (Fig. S3, Supporting Information). Under the carefully chosen experimental stings, the PANI/CQDs films achieved an optimal state and underwent additional thorough physicochemical examinations.

Analyzing the TEM imagery, the synthesized CQDs appear as spherical dots with diameters ranging from 3 to 10 nm (Fig. 1 (a)). Notably, the CQDs exhibit a lattice distance of 0.338 nm, aligning with the (002) diffraction planes characteristic of graphite [37]. The TEM images of PANI/CQDs composite films corroborate the incorporation of CQDs into the matrix (Fig. 1(b)). Additionally, the enlarged depiction of the highlighted area (Fig. 1(c)) displays lattice spacing of 0.34 nm and 0.82 nm, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphite and the (001) plane of PANI, respectively [37]. Consistent with the TEM findings, the XRD spectrum of CQDs (Fig. 1(j)) features a broad peak centered at 26°, indicative of the (002) crystal planes of graphite structure [33].

The surface morphologies of PANI and PANI/CQDs films are depicted in Fig. 1 (d, e) and Fig. S5 (Supporting Information). The PANI-5 and PANI/CQDs-5 porous films display a reticular framework, which facilitates the penetration of electrolytes and transportation of ions. As illustrated in Fig. S5, certain aggregated particles adhere to the surface of prepared films, a phenomenon associated with the polymerization process of PANI films [38]. Consequently, the number of particles on surface films rises in tandem with the polymerization charge. Notably, the quantity of the aggregated particles detected on the surface of PANI/CQDs films is less than that observed on pure PANI films. This discrepancy likely stems from the electrostatic and π-π interactions between CQDs and PANI, which can effectively inhibit the agglomeration of the PANI particles [39]. The AFM images for both PANI-5 and PANI/CQDs-5 films, each spanning a 5 × 5 μm2 area, are presented in Fig. S6. The respective surface roughness Ra measurements reveal values of 46.9 nm for PANI and 87.5 nm for PANI/CQDs films. The porosity of PANI/CQDs-5 film (9.9 %) is more than twice as large as PANI-5 film (4.1 %). In contrast to the compact surface of PANI-5 film, the PANI/CQDs-5 film exhibits a rougher and looser disordered in-plane structure. This may be attributed to decreasing the degree of order in-plane PANI with the introduction of CQDs. In the meanwhile, all films present uniform lamellar micropore structures, with a thickness of almost 40 μm and a firm adhesion to the substrate membrane. This uniformity stems from the tetrahedral structure of SA, which facilitates the formation of ionic and hydrogen bonds with adjacent molecular chains. Consequently, upon electrodeposition on an Au electrode, these bonds promote the creation of a highly ordered molecular chain within the oriented lamellar PANI crystals [20].

XRD analysis provides further evidence of the transformation in the molecular chain arrangement of PANI, induced by CQDs. As shown in Fig. 1(j), all films display a distinct and characteristic diffraction peak at 2θ = 10°, corresponding to the (001) plane of PANI [37,40]. Utilizing Bragg's formula, the inter-layer spacing of PANI and PANI/CQDs are calculated to be 8.89 Å and 8.16 Å, respectively, consistent with the findings from TEM measurements. Results confirmed that the highly ordered lamellar structure of PANI by doping of SA, the smaller spacing value of PANI/CQDs film may result from π-π electron stacking between PANI and CQDs [41], which can facilitate a reduction in the vertical direction ion transmission distance. Additionally, the peaks observed at 2θ = 21° and 25° correspond to the periodicity aligned with the PANI chains and perpendicular to the polymer backbone chain, indicating diffraction from the (010) and (100) planes [40]. Following the introduction of CQDs, the intensity of the peak at 21° in PANI/CQDs decreases, while the peak at 25° increases and shifts to 26°. This indicates a reduction in the level of ordering within the plane of PANI and an enhancement perpendicular to PANI. It indicates that interactions between CQDs and molecular chains of PANI may break the ordered molecular interactions between PANI and SA [20]. In addition, the SAED pattern shows no obvious diffraction rings of PANI/CQDs (Fig. 1(k)), suggesting the disordered structure of PANI/CQDs in-plane.

The UV–vis absorption profiles of CQDs and PANI/CQDs are presented in Fig. 1(l). The prominent peak at 235 nm is linked to the π-π∗ electron transition in C=C bonds, while the signal at 345 nm is attributed to n-π∗ transitions within the C=O group [41]. This indicates the presence of oxygen functional groups in the structure of the obtained CQDs. In the case of PANI/CQDs composite, the observed signals confirmed both PANI and CQDs structure. A strong absorption band at 238 nm confirms the presence of CQDs and PANI through π-π∗ electrons transition in C=C bonds. The less pronounced peak at 276 nm is indicative of n-π∗ transitions in the C=C group. Variations in signal positions compared to those in reference materials can be attributed to interactions between CQDs and PANI.

FTIR analysis was conducted to illustrate the molecular structures of composite films, as shown in Fig. 2 (a). In the FTIR spectra of CQDs, a distinctive peak at 1530 cm−1 was observed, indicating the presence of a typical graphene structure composed of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. The FTIR spectrum obtained for the PANI/CQDs composite film exhibited signals from both the CQDs and the PANI-5 structure. For example, double bands around 3580 and 3100 cm−1, are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of the O–H bond peak (3710 cm−1) from the CQDs and the N–H bond peak (3280 cm−1) from the PANI-5 structure. These peaks shift to smaller wavenumbers, suggesting the hydrogen-bond interaction between the hydroxyl group and -NH groups in the composites [42]. The peaks observed at 1620 and 1480 cm−1 are related to the stretching vibration modes C=C of the quinone diamine and benzenoid diamine units. The characteristic peaks at 1318 and 1186 cm−1, as well as 1124 cm−1, can be attributed to the C-N stretching vibration of benzenoid, and N = Q = N vibration of quinoidal units, respectively. The additional peak at 1520 cm−1 is a result of interactions between oxygen functional groups of CQDs (1700, 1130 cm−1) and PANI [43]. All these signals confirm the presence of both components in the PANI/CQDs-5 film.

The results from FTIR measurements were further supported by Raman spectra of the films, as shown in Fig. 2(b). The CQDs displayed characteristic peaks at 1335 and 1575 cm−1, corresponding to the D band and G band of signals typical for carbon nanostructure. The D band at 1335 cm−1 signifies the presence of sp3 defects, whereas the G band at 1575 cm−1 represents the in-plane vibration of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. The atomic ratio of sp3/sp2, expressed as the relative intensity of the D and G band (I D/I G), reflects the degree of disorder. With a calculated I D/I G ratio of 0.63, it can be concluded that CQDs possess highly crystalline and graphitic structures.

The peaks at 1622 and 1580 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of the benzenoid ring (C-C) and quinoid ring (C=C), respectively. The peaks at 1485 cm−1 and 1175 cm−1 are related to the stretching vibration of the C=N bond in the quinoid ring and the C-N bond in the quinoid ring, respectively [44]. The spectrum of PANI/CQDs-5 shows a shift at 1597 cm−1, and a significantly enhanced peak at 1485 cm−1, indicating interaction between the graphene structure of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms in the CQDs and PANI chains. The shoulder peak at 1315 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of more delocalized polaronic structures (C-N•+) [44]. Compared with the Raman spectrum of the PANI porous film, the peaks of PANI/CQDs-5 film shift to a higher frequency (1342 cm−1), indicating that the presence of sp3 defects in the CQDs leads to the delocalization of numerous polarons and bipolarons along the PANI chains.

The polarons and bipolarons, as the main carriers of PANI film, are crucial factors in regulating the variable IR emissivity (ɛ). Thus, it is necessary to confirm the number and state of polarons and bipolarons for the prepared films. The surface composition and elements for the prepared films are measured by XPS. An additional peak located at 290.2 eV can be ascribed to π-π∗ vibrations satellite [45] of the PANI/CQDs-5 films, consistent with the Raman spectra. The stacking of the π electron cloud between graphite and the benzenoid/quinoid structure in PANI contributes to the enhanced electron transport and electrochemical performance of the PANI/CQDs-5 films. Simultaneously, the N 1s spectra of PANI-5 and PANI/CQDs-5 films are decomposed into three peaks, referring to -NH- (399.5 eV), -NH+•- (400.7 eV), and -NH+- (401.6 eV), respectively [46]. Notably, the components at binding energies exceeding 400 eV are attributed to N atoms with delocalized polarons and positively charged bipolarons [47]. Thus, in case of PANI/CQDs-5 film, the N 1s peak shows a shift to higher binding energies, and the combined percentage of -NH+•- and -NH+- moieties from 69 % rises to 76 %. It indicates an increase in the number of polarons and bipolarons (Fig. S11, Supporting Information). This phenomenon confirms that the CQDs interact with the PANI chains in the composite films, thereby enhancing the oxidation state and the degree of protonation.

The findings reveal that hydrogen bonding between oxygen functional groups of CQDs and PANI promotes the alignment of PANI into lamellar structures, characterized by in-plane short-range disorder, thereby augmenting the contact surface and reactivity sites within PANI/CQDs films. Additionally, the π-π electron stacking between graphene and aromatic/quinoid components of PANI potentially accelerates electron transfer, ultimately bolstering the electrochemical performance of the composite material [41].

The charge transfer and ion diffusion behaviors of the PANI/CQDs porous films were demonstrated by CV and EIS tests in 0.5 M SA solutions. Fig. 3 (a–c) depicts the CV profiles at a scan velocity of 50 mV/s within a potential window spanning −0.3 V–1.2 V. All samples display two distinct redox couples, indicative of reversible transitions from leucoemeraldine base (LB) to emeraldine salt (ES), and subsequently from ES to pernigraniline base (PNB) [48]. Notably, the PANI/CQDs film exhibits significantly enhanced redox peak currents compared to the pure PANI film, suggesting its superior electrochemical performance. This enhancement is attributed to the expansive surface area of CQDs, fostering greater interfacial contact between PANI and the electrolyte, thereby facilitating charge transfer processes. With the increase in the polymerization charge, the CV curve areas of the prepared films enlarge, and the oxidation peaks shift negatively. Compared with PANI-5, the PANI/CQDs-5 porous film shows a larger electrochemical active area and lower energy consumption, suggesting better electrochemical performance.

To verify the electron conduction and ion transportation through the electrode/electrolyte interfaces, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy is measured. As shown in Fig. 3 (d) and Fig. S12, the Nyquist plots of PANI/CQDs films exhibit a distinct sunk and smaller semicircle pattern at higher frequencies. This feature signifies a reduced charge-transfer resistance (Rct) [49,50]. The line portion at the lower frequency region, characterized by a steeper slope, is ascribed to the Warburg diffusion, indicative of accelerated ion diffusion within the PANI/CQDs-5 film [51]. Moreover, the Nyquist plots adhere to an equivalent Randles circuit model (inset of Fig. 3 (d)). Calculations reveal Rct values of 17.33 Ω for PANI-5 and 4.67 Ω for PANI/CQDs-5, respectively (Table S2, Supporting Information). The smaller Rct value observed for PANI/CQDs-5 underscores a more efficient electron transfer process from the electrolyte to the film during the redox [52,53].

To reveal why the superior ion transfer from the electrolyte is received by PANI/CQDs, the H-ion diffusion kinetics by DFT calculations were analyzed further. Fig. 4 (a) and (b) show the H-ion diffusion path for PANI and PANI/CQDs, respectively, with the energy profiles of H-ion migration for the two structures displayed in Fig. 4 (c). The computational results demonstrate that the migration barriers are 0.36 (PANI) and 0.27 eV (PANI/CQDs), respectively, indicating faster ion diffusion for PANI/CQDs as shown in the experiment results. The PANI exhibiting a laminar structure and diminished interlayer spacing could potentially decrease the ion transport distance vertically. In addition, the determined E
ads value stands at −17.56 eV, a negative value figure that augurs well for the adsorption process. It is surmised that the primary interaction mechanism revolves around hydrogen bonds, with a minor role played by π-π stacking interactions [54]. With short-range disorder surface and rough polyporous morphology, the PANI/CQDs film presents better electrochemical properties.

It has been demonstrated that the electrochromic properties of PANI are intimately linked to its distinctive redox process, which can be manipulated by applying voltages [46]. In this study, potentials of −0.25 V and 0.55 V are utilized, taking into account that these values enable the films to undergo a complete transition between their LB (bleached) and ES (colored) states, along with a quantitative assessment of their thermal radiation modulation capability. The investigation of Fourier transform IR spectra encompassed a wavelength range from 2.5 to 25 μm, with the ε values derived utilizing Eqs. (S1) and (S2) (Supporting Information). Fig. 5 visually presents the emittance profiles of PANI/CQDs and PANI films, each subjected to varying degrees of polymerization charges under applied voltages of −0.25 V and 0.55 V. The emittance variation (Δε), which denotes the difference between emittance in the LB state (ε-0.25V) and the ES state (ε 0.55V), is shown in Table 1 . It should be noted that the emittance values are averaged across the whole band, which spans from 3 to 5 μm, 8–14 μm and 2.5–25 μm.

As shown in Fig. 5 and Table 1, the value of Δε for the prepared films gradually rises as the polymerization charges increase, yet it diminishes upon reaching 0.6 C. This pattern aligns with the polarons and bipolarons identified in the XPS findings. The PANI/CQDs-5 films present a notably elevated value of Δε, demonstrating a range of emittance variations spanning from 0.48 to 0.90 (Δε = 0.423) across 2.5–25 μm, 0.24 to 0.65 (Δε = 0.41) within 3–5 μm, and 0.22 to 0.68 (Δε = 0.46) in the 8–14 μm region, respectively. The PANI/CQDs porous film, especially the PANI/CQDs-5 film has superior modulation ability to tailor the thermal radiation. To realize better emissivity modulation, it is an effective strategy to regulate conversion between various states for PANI films [38]. The PANI/CQDs films display relatively loose and microporous surface structures with the incorporation of CQDs. The small interface resistance of PANI/CQDs films between the surface and the underlying dense layer facilitates ion insertion/extraction, accelerating the reversible transitions of the PANI/CQDs film between its ES and LE configurations. Additionally, the radiation powers of the prepared films at different voltages were calculated according to equation [38] (Eq. S(1), Supporting Information), and the results are shown in Fig. S13 (Supporting Information). The emissive power of PANI-5 and PANI/CQDs-5 films obtain modulation of emissive power as 85 W/m2 and 161 W/m2 in 2.5–25 μm, respectively.

To gain deeper insights into the electrochromic properties exhibited by the PANI/CQDs film, an investigation was conducted on its observed color alteration within a three-electrode configuration. The setup employed 0.5 M H2SO4 as the electrolyte, with a graphite rod serving as the counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode. As depicted in Fig. 6 (a) , the film demonstrates reversible color changes, transitioning through yellow, chartreuse, green, and atrovirens hues, in response to voltage changes from −0.5 V to 0.5 V. Fig. 6 (b) illustrates the corresponding reflectance spectra of the PANI/CQDs film, which are contingent upon the application of voltages varying between −0.5 and 0.5 V. The optical contrast (ΔT) arises from the film's non-transparent substrate, representing the variation in reflectance between its colored and the bleached state. Notably, the PANI/CQDs film exhibits a peak ΔT of 53 % at 850 nm, surpassing the 38 % recorded for pure PANI (Fig. S14 (a), Supporting Information). Inspection reveals that the film, whether in its bleached or LB state, processes heightened reflectance, which diminishes markedly across the 300–2000 nm wavelength spectrum as voltages escalate. Specifically, the PANI/CQDs film displays a reflection peak at 640 nm under −0.5 V, aligned with the yellow film visible in Fig. 6 (a). As voltage increases, this reflection peak shifts towards shorter wavelengths. Furthermore, the distinct color transitions exhibited by the film can be demarcated through their positioning within the CIELAB color space (based on CIE1931 standards), as illustrated in Fig. 6(c). It is noteworthy that the PANI/CQDs film displays a sequential transformation from yellow through green to atrovirens, showcasing its versatile color modulation capability under varying voltage conditions.

To evaluate the response time of the prepared films, the Chronoamperometric (CA) curves were measured as shown in Fig. 6 (c). The response time, another key parameter for electrochromic materials, is defined as the time to achieve 90 % change in the total current density between bleached states (bias potential: −0.25 V) and steady colored (bias potential: 0.55 V) [55]. Upon application of a positive voltage shift, the film transitions from golden yellow to dark green for both PANI-5 and PANI/CQDs-5. Benefited from the smaller charge transfer resistance and faster ion transport, PANI/CQDs-5 films exhibit coloration and bleaching times of 2.5 s and 3.6 s, respectively, which are reduced compared to the 4.3 s and 5.7 s times observed in PANI-5 films. As depicted in Fig. 6(d), the endurance of PANI/CQDs was assessed through CA measurements, indicating that it maintains over 95 % of its peak performance even after 100 cycles.

The comparison of IR emissivity modulation ability and cycling stability of PANI-based thin films is reported in the literature [38,55–60], and this work is shown in Fig. S14(b). As mentioned above, the IR emissivity modulation capability is quantified by Δε. The results are superior to other PANI electrochromic films. Reported CSA-PANI films can achieve stronger IR modulation capability in a specific wavelength band, such as 0.559 in 3–5 μm [59]. The HClO4-PANI electrochromic device can maintain over 95 % stability after 100 cycles [60]. In general, the PANI/CQDs porous films present enhanced electrochromic performance and excellent IR tunability.

In conclusion, the PANI/CQDs porous films were prepared and investigated by the electro-polymerization process on the Au/PES porous substrates. The films exhibit an oriented lamellar structure induced by dopants of sulfuric acid with tetrahedral structure. Dopants with oxygen functional groups of the CQDs can induce the formation of the hydrogen bond interactions between CQDs and PANI chains, which is beneficial for the formation of short-range disordered structures. These unique structural with long-range orderliness and short-range disorder facilitates shorter diffusion pathways and larger surface areas for electrolyte-film interactions. Based on loose and microporous structure, the PANI/CQDs-5 porous film shows excellent electrochromic properties and IR modulation ability, achieving a ΔT of 53 % at 850 nm, and with Δε of 0.41, 0.46 and 0.42 in 3–5 μm, 8–14 μm and 2.5–25 μm, respectively. The response times are 2.5 and 3.6 s in the process of coloration and bleaching, respectively. Furthermore, the PANI/CQDs-5 films can maintain over 95 % of their current cycling durability after 100 cycles. This work provides a new insight to enhance the electrochromic performance of PANI films.

Ting Qin: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Lianwen Deng: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Ningrong Zhang: Supervision, Methodology. Pin Zhang: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Yawen Liu: Methodology, Funding acquisition. Longlong Qin: Methodology, Data curation. Shengxiang Huang: Supervision, Methodology. Leilei Qiu: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Sen Peng: Methodology, Data curation.
Data will be made available on request.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Laboratory on Electromagnetic Environmental Effects and Electro-optical Engineering (NO. KYGYGZSY2213), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21902186 and 21905305).
